title: Keep source order aligned with meaning
principle: Author DOM order from reading and focus logic, then use layout for visual arrangement without changing meaning.
rationale: CSS reordering can make visual order diverge from keyboard and assistive-technology navigation, producing an incoherent task sequence.
applies_when: Grid, flexbox, sticky regions, or responsive rearrangement changes visual placement.
exceptions: Purely decorative elements may move independently when hidden from the accessibility tree.
implementation: Write a linear content outline first.
Prefer reflow and grid-area changes that preserve meaningful DOM order.
verification: Disable CSS and confirm the document remains understandable.
Tab and reading order follow the expected sequence at every breakpoint.
accessibility: Do not use positive tabindex or CSS order to repair an illogical source.
responsive: Test reordering at every layout transition, not just endpoints.
performance: A semantic flow often reduces duplicate responsive markup.
browser_notes: Verify grid and contents-related accessibility behavior in supported engines.

title: Build hierarchy with rhythm before containers
principle: Use proximity, alignment, whitespace, and type hierarchy to group content before adding borders, backgrounds, or cards.
rationale: Container-first layouts create visual noise and nested boxes, while rhythm communicates relationships with less chrome.
applies_when: Grouping sections, metadata, controls, or repeated content.
exceptions: A boundary is useful when interaction, selection, drag behavior, or contrasting surface semantics require it.
implementation: Start in grayscale with spacing and alignment only.
Add a container only when it clarifies a real boundary.
verification: Removing decorative borders does not destroy grouping.
Adjacent spacing consistently reflects semantic distance.
accessibility: Groups that need programmatic relationships use semantic elements, fieldsets, or labels—not visual proximity alone.
responsive: Spacing scales compress deliberately without collapsing group boundaries.
performance: Prefer simple layout properties over effect-heavy wrappers.
browser_notes: Avoid relying on subpixel hairlines as the only group cue.

title: Set line measure and density by reading task
principle: Tune width, spacing, and density to the task: comfortable measure for sustained reading and compact, aligned density for repeated comparison.
rationale: A universal spacious layout wastes scan distance in tools, while dense prose increases fatigue and loses place.
applies_when: Choosing content width, row height, section spacing, or dashboard density.
exceptions: User-selectable density may support expert tools when each mode remains accessible.
implementation: Set a readable text measure and a distinct data-comparison grid.
Use optical alignment and tabular numerals where comparison benefits.
verification: Users can scan repeated values without excessive eye travel.
Long-form text does not span the full wide viewport.
accessibility: Density does not reduce target size, text size, or separation below accessible minima.
responsive: At narrow widths, recompose comparisons rather than shrinking text.
performance: For very large dense datasets, evaluate pagination or virtualization without breaking accessibility.
browser_notes: Verify zoom and text-only zoom do not cause overlap or loss.

title: Make negative space earn its role
principle: Minimalism is selective emphasis and deliberate rhythm, not undifferentiated vacancy: each major gap should clarify hierarchy, grouping, pacing, focus, or an interaction boundary.
rationale: Empty scale can mimic polish in a screenshot while withholding proof, next steps, and relationships; purposeful space instead makes the remaining content easier to understand and act on.
applies_when: Choosing a sparse, low-chrome, spacious, premium, or editorial composition.
A first viewport uses large vertical gaps, oversized type, or intentionally limited content.
exceptions: A cinematic, cultural, or editorial pause may be spacious when distinctive content or art direction earns the pacing.
Consequential or dense tasks may intentionally minimize expressive space in favor of immediate information and recovery.
implementation: Assign every major gap a job before styling: separate meanings, improve reading measure, stage a decision, frame evidence, or create a deliberate narrative beat.
Pair spacious composition with specific content, visible affordances, and a purposeful rhythm; do not use fixed viewport height or blank bands as a substitute for hierarchy.
verification: At short laptop and mobile viewports, useful context, proof, or the next action appears without traversing a purposeless void.
Reviewers can name the job of major gaps and the semantic relationship that each one clarifies.
accessibility: Use headings, landmarks, labels, and DOM order for relationships; visual distance alone must not carry essential grouping or instruction.
responsive: Clamp, compress, or recompose gaps at constrained widths and heights instead of preserving a fixed empty band.
performance: Do not load decorative media or force viewport-sized layout solely to manufacture empty scale.
browser_notes: Test dynamic mobile viewport changes and zoom so space does not obscure actions or create false end-of-page cues.

title: Assign typography by semantic role
principle: Define a small set of semantic text roles whose size, weight, line height, and spacing jointly express hierarchy.
rationale: Role-based typography remains consistent across pages and prevents ad hoc font sizes from becoming a substitute for content structure.
applies_when: Creating a type scale or styling any repeated text category.
exceptions: A campaign display treatment may extend the system when its scope and fallback are documented.
implementation: Define roles such as display, section heading, body, label, support, and numeric data.
Use no more hierarchy levels than the content structure needs.
verification: The same role has the same purpose across components.
Heading semantics are chosen independently from visual size.
accessibility: Body and control text remain legible at zoom and user font overrides.
responsive: Use fluid or stepped roles with bounded sizes and stable hierarchy.
performance: Limit font families, weights, and subsets to those used.
browser_notes: Provide robust fallback metrics to reduce layout shift.

title: Control measure, leading, and paragraph rhythm
principle: Keep sustained-reading lines within a comfortable measure, use leading appropriate to size and width, and make paragraph spacing predictable.
rationale: Readable measure reduces horizontal searching, while adequate leading helps users retain their place across lines.
applies_when: Rendering paragraphs, instructions, legal text, articles, or documentation.
exceptions: Short labels, data cells, and display headlines follow task-specific measures.
implementation: Cap prose inline size using character-relative units.
Test actual content at default and enlarged text settings.
verification: Prose does not become edge-to-edge on wide screens.
At 200% zoom, content reflows without horizontal reading scroll.
accessibility: User text spacing overrides do not clip, overlap, or hide content.
responsive: Padding and measure adapt together so narrow screens retain usable line length.
performance: Reserve font space and preload only critical fonts.
browser_notes: Test variable-font axes and fallback wrapping across engines.

title: Make typography resilient during font loading
principle: Text must remain readable immediately and layouts must tolerate fallback-font metrics without destructive shifts.
rationale: Fonts are network resources; blocking or unstable font swaps delay meaning and can move controls while a user acts.
exceptions: A brand mark rendered as an image is not body text but still needs an accessible name.
implementation: Use an appropriate font-display strategy, subset by language, and tune fallback metrics where needed.
Avoid icon fonts for essential controls.
verification: Throttle or block font requests and complete the primary task.
accessibility: Text is never invisible for an unbounded period and custom fonts remain legible under user preferences.
responsive: Fallback metrics do not cause narrow layouts to clip actions.
performance: Preload only critical files; remove unused families, weights, styles, and glyph ranges.
browser_notes: Verify font format, unicode-range, and metric override support against the browser baseline.

title: Use color roles, not isolated swatches
principle: Define color by semantic role and interaction state across surfaces, text, borders, focus, selection, and feedback.
rationale: Role-based palettes preserve meaning across themes and stop color choices from drifting component by component.
applies_when: Creating themes, brand palettes, state colors, or component styles.
exceptions: Data visualization may use a separate tested categorical or sequential scale.
implementation: Map primitives to semantic roles and state pairs.
Document foreground/background combinations rather than testing colors in isolation.
verification: Every used combination passes its required contrast target.
Dark, light, forced-colors, and interactive states retain meaning.
accessibility: Never use color as the only cue for state, error, selection, or chart meaning.
responsive: Color meaning remains stable across reflow and condensed variants.
performance: Prefer tokenized CSS values over duplicated theme payloads.
browser_notes: Test forced-colors and system color behavior in supporting browsers.

title: Pair color with a redundant cue
principle: Communicate distinctions with text, shape, position, pattern, iconography, or programmatic state in addition to color.
rationale: Color perception varies and colors can disappear in forced-color, low-quality display, glare, or grayscale conditions.
applies_when: Color conveys error, status, category, selection, availability, or interactivity.
exceptions: Purely decorative color variation carries no information.
implementation: Add a concise label or structural cue.
For charts, provide direct labels or an accessible data representation.
verification: The distinction remains clear in grayscale and forced-colors mode.
Assistive technology receives equivalent state text.
accessibility: Satisfies non-color communication and supports color-vision differences.
responsive: Redundant labels are not removed at narrow widths if that erases meaning.
performance: Use lightweight CSS or inline vector cues rather than decorative image assets.
browser_notes: Verify system color overrides do not make icons or borders disappear.
